steps.title: Server project scaffolding
steps.goal: Initialize the Rust/Axum server project with configuration, database connection pool, project structure, and health check endpoint.
tasks: Initialize Rust project with Cargo; add axum, tokio, sqlx (PostgreSQL), serde, dotenvy dependencies
Create project directory structure: routes/, services/, models/, db/, middleware/
Implement configuration loading from environment variables (database URL, JWT secret, server bind address)
Set up PostgreSQL connection pool using sqlx
Implement GET /health endpoint returning 200 OK
Structure codebase for future i18n (error messages as constants/enums, not inline strings)
definition_of_done: Server compiles, starts, and responds to GET /health with 200 OK
Database connection pool connects to PostgreSQL successfully
Configuration loads from environment variables or .env file

steps.title: Database schema: users and authentication
steps.goal: Create the database tables for user accounts, credentials, social login links, and refresh tokens.
tasks: Create migration: users table (id UUID PK, email unique, username, password_hash nullable, created_at, updated_at)
Create migration: social_logins table (user_id FK, provider enum Google/Apple, provider_user_id, created_at; unique on provider + provider_user_id)
Create migration: refresh_tokens table (id UUID, user_id FK, token_hash, expires_at, revoked_at nullable)
Create migration: password_reset_tokens table (id UUID, user_id FK, token_hash, expires_at, used_at nullable)
Define corresponding Rust model structs with sqlx FromRow derives
definition_of_done: All migrations run successfully against a clean database
Model structs compile and can be queried with sqlx
Unique constraints enforced on email and social login provider+provider_user_id

steps.title: Database schema: graph and OSM data
steps.goal: Create the database tables for storing the routable graph derived from OpenStreetMap, with PostGIS geometry, pgRouting topology, and edge ID stability design.
tasks: Enable PostGIS and pgRouting extensions in migration
Create migration: osm_regions table (id, region_key unique, bbox geometry, imported_at, osm_data_timestamp)
Create migration: nodes table (id BIGINT PK, geom geometry(Point, 4326), region_id FK)

Create migration: edges table (id BIGINT PK, stable_edge_id VARCHAR unique for cross-refresh stability, source_node BIGINT FK, target_node BIGINT FK, geom geometry(LineString, 4326), length_m DOUBLE, is_dead_end BOOLEAN, region_id FK, osm_way_id BIGINT, osm_node_pair_hash for stable ID matching)
Add spatial indexes on edges.geom and nodes.geom
Create pgRouting topology or equivalent index structure for shortest-path queries
Design and document the edge ID stability strategy (hash of sorted OSM node pair + way ID)
definition_of_done: PostGIS and pgRouting extensions are active
All tables created with spatial indexes
Edge ID stability strategy documented and encoded in schema
pgRouting can perform shortest-path queries on the edges table

steps.title: Database schema: walk history
steps.goal: Create tables for recording per-user walk sessions and per-edge walk counts.
tasks: Create migration: walk_sessions table (id UUID PK, user_id FK, started_at, ended_at nullable, status enum active/completed/abandoned, total_distance_m DOUBLE, route_mode enum circular/a_to_b/a_to_b_shortest, planned_route_geom geometry nullable)
Create migration: edge_walk_counts table (user_id FK, edge_stable_id VARCHAR, walk_count INT default 0, last_walked_at, PK on user_id + edge_stable_id)
Add index on edge_walk_counts(user_id) for efficient per-user lookups
Define Rust model structs
definition_of_done: Migrations run successfully
edge_walk_counts supports efficient lookup of all edge counts for a given user
walk_sessions tracks individual walk lifecycle with status transitions

steps.title: Database schema: user preferences
steps.goal: Create tables for user-configurable settings and per-path preferences.
tasks: Create migration: user_settings table (user_id FK PK, margin_percent INT default 10, shortest_path_margin_percent INT default 10, dead_ends_enabled BOOLEAN default false, circularity_weight FLOAT default 0.5)
Create migration: user_path_preferences table (user_id FK, edge_stable_id VARCHAR, preference enum prefer_not/block, created_at, PK on user_id + edge_stable_id)
Define Rust model structs
definition_of_done: Migrations run successfully
Settings table has sensible defaults for all fields
Path preferences support prefer_not and block per edge per user

steps.title: Database schema: subscriptions and payments
steps.goal: Create tables for tracking user subscription status, free tier usage, and payment audit log.
tasks: Create migration: subscriptions table (user_id FK PK, tier enum free/paid default free, total_walked_distance_m DOUBLE default 0, free_tier_limit_m DOUBLE default 100000, subscription_start TIMESTAMP nullable, subscription_end TIMESTAMP nullable, payment_provider enum mollie/apple/google nullable, external_subscription_id VARCHAR nullable)
Create migration: payment_events table (id UUID PK, user_id FK, event_type VARCHAR, provider VARCHAR, external_id VARCHAR, amount_cents INT, currency VARCHAR, created_at)
Define Rust model structs
definition_of_done: Migrations run successfully
Free tier limit defaults to 100000m (100km)
Subscription and payment event records queryable per user

steps.title: OSM data ingestion pipeline
steps.goal: Build a pipeline that imports OpenStreetMap data into the graph tables with stable edge IDs, on-demand regional loading, and dead end detection.
steps.input: Graph schema with edge ID stability design (step 3)
tasks: Implement OSM PBF file parser or integrate osm2pgsql/osm4routing for extracting walkable ways
Filter to walkable paths only (footways, paths, sidewalks, pedestrian areas, residential streets, tracks)
Generate stable edge IDs from OSM way ID + sorted node pair hash
Implement region-based import: define region boundaries, import one region at a time, record in osm_regions
Handle cross-border edges that span region boundaries
Mark dead-end edges (edges leading to nodes with degree 1)
Implement re-import that preserves stable edge IDs across OSM data refreshes
Build pgRouting topology after import
definition_of_done: A test region imports successfully with all walkable edges
Edge IDs remain stable across a re-import of the same region with updated OSM data
Dead-end edges are correctly identified
pgRouting shortest-path queries work on imported data
Cross-region edge continuity is maintained

steps.title: Self-hosted map tile server
steps.goal: Deploy a self-hosted tile server that renders map tiles from OSM data for the Flutter client.
tasks: Select and configure tile server software (e.g., Martin, tileserver-gl, or OpenMapTiles)
Set up tile generation pipeline from imported OSM data
Configure tile serving with appropriate caching headers
Ensure tile coverage matches the same regions as the graph data
Configure CORS headers for web client access
definition_of_done: Tile server serves raster or vector tiles over HTTP
Tiles render correctly in a browser-based test map viewer
Tile coverage matches imported OSM regions
CORS allows cross-origin requests from the web client

steps.title: Authentication service: email and password
steps.goal: Implement user registration, login, JWT access tokens, and refresh token rotation for email/password authentication.
steps.input: Users and auth schema (step 2), user settings and subscription tables (steps 5-6)
tasks: Implement password hashing with argon2
Implement registration: validate email uniqueness, hash password, create user record, initialize default user_settings and free-tier subscription records
Implement login: verify email + password, issue short-lived JWT access token and refresh token
Implement token refresh: validate refresh token, issue new access + refresh token pair, revoke old refresh token
Implement logout: revoke refresh token
Create JWT extraction and validation middleware for protecting routes
definition_of_done: User can register with email and password
User can log in and receive JWT access token + refresh token
JWT middleware rejects invalid and expired tokens
Refresh token rotation works and old tokens are revoked on use
Registration initializes default settings and free-tier subscription

steps.title: Authentication service: social login
steps.goal: Add Google OAuth2 and Apple OIDC sign-in flows to the authentication service.
tasks: Implement Google OAuth2 flow: receive authorization code from client, exchange for tokens, extract user info, create or link account
Implement Apple OIDC flow: validate identity token, extract user info, create or link account
Handle account linking: if email already registered, link social login to existing account
Issue same JWT + refresh token pair as email/password login
Store provider and provider_user_id in social_logins table
definition_of_done: User can sign in with Google and receive JWT
User can sign in with Apple and receive JWT
Social login creates a new account or links to an existing account matching the email
Subsequent social logins for the same provider user return the same account

steps.title: Account management service
steps.goal: Implement password reset flow and immediate account deletion with complete data purge.
steps.input: Auth service (steps 9-10), all user data tables (steps 2-6)
tasks: Implement password reset request: generate token, store hash in password_reset_tokens, send reset email
Implement password reset confirmation: validate token, update password hash, mark token used

Implement account deletion: require explicit confirmation, then immediately and permanently delete all user data across all tables (user, social_logins, tokens, walk_sessions, edge_walk_counts, user_settings, user_path_preferences, subscriptions, payment_events)
Ensure deletion flow is identical for email/password and social login users
Return clear response stating deletion is immediate and permanent before executing
definition_of_done: Password reset flow works end-to-end (request, email, confirm, new password)
Account deletion removes all user data from every table
Deletion is immediate upon confirmation with no deferred processing
Both email/password and social login users can delete their accounts identically

steps.title: Walk history service
steps.goal: Implement the service layer for managing walk sessions and recording per-user per-edge walk counts.
tasks: Implement walk session creation (start walk with route mode and planned route geometry)
Implement edge walk count incrementing: given a list of walked edge stable IDs, increment counts in edge_walk_counts
Implement walk session completion (mark as completed, store total distance)
Implement walk session abandonment (manual early end, partial data preserved)
Implement query: get all edge walk counts for a user within a bounding box (for planner and heatmap)
Implement total walked distance query for a user (for free tier tracking)
Implement interrupted session recovery: accept and merge delayed partial walk data
definition_of_done: Walk session lifecycle works: create, update, complete, abandon
Edge walk counts increment correctly per edge per user
Walk count query returns per-edge history for a user filtered by bounding box
Total distance aggregation is accurate across all sessions

steps.title: User preferences service
steps.goal: Implement CRUD operations for user settings and per-path preferences with penalty calculation.
steps.input: User preferences schema (step 5), walk history service for max walk count (step 12)
tasks: Implement get and update user settings (margin, shortest_path_margin, dead_ends_enabled, circularity_weight)
Implement set path preference: store prefer_not or block for a given edge_stable_id
Implement remove path preference (undo prefer_not or block)
Implement prefer_not penalty calculation: max(10, user's highest single-edge walk count in history)
Implement query: get all path preferences for a user, optionally filtered by bounding box
definition_of_done: Settings can be read and updated per user
Path preferences can be set, queried, and removed
Prefer_not penalty correctly uses max(10, user's maximum single-edge walk count)
Before any walk history exists, prefer_not penalty defaults to 10

steps.title: Diversity scoring engine
steps.goal: Implement the Fibonacci-based diversity penalty calculation for route candidate edges.
steps.input: Walk history service (step 12), user preferences service (step 13)
tasks: Implement Fibonacci sequence lookup (index = times walked): 0→0, 1→1, 2→1, 3→2, 4→3, 5→5, 6→8, ...
Implement per-edge penalty: edge_length_m × fibonacci(walk_count)
Apply prefer_not multiplier for edges with prefer_not preference
Exclude blocked edges entirely from candidate set
Implement total route diversity penalty as sum of all per-edge penalties
Define and implement normalization of diversity score to a standard scale for weighting against circularity
definition_of_done: Fibonacci mapping is correct for walk counts 0 through at least 20
Edge penalty correctly multiplies edge distance by Fibonacci of walk count
Prefer_not and block preferences are correctly applied
Diversity score is normalized to a defined scale

steps.title: Circularity scoring engine
steps.goal: Implement circularity scoring that measures how evenly a route spreads around its geometric center, with normalization for weighting.
tasks: Compute geometric center of all route waypoints including the start point
Measure angular distribution and radial variance of waypoints around the center
Score circularity: a perfect circle scores maximum, an out-and-back route scores near zero
Define and implement normalization of circularity score to the same scale as diversity score
Implement configurable weighting: combine circularity and diversity scores using the user's circularity_weight slider value
definition_of_done: A roughly circular route scores high circularity
An out-and-back route scores near zero circularity
Circularity and diversity are normalized to the same scale
The weighting slider has meaningful effect across its full range

steps.title: Route planner: circular mode
steps.goal: Implement circular route generation that produces loop routes optimized for diversity and circularity within the distance margin.
steps.input: Diversity scoring (step 14), circularity scoring (step 15), graph data (step 7)
tasks: Implement start point snapping to nearest routable node using spatial query
Implement loop route search algorithm using pgRouting or custom graph traversal that returns to start
Optimize route for combined diversity penalty (minimize) and circularity score (maximize) using configured weights
Enforce distance constraint: total route distance within requested distance ± user's margin percentage
Return route as ordered list of edge stable IDs, GeoJSON geometry, total distance, and individual scores
definition_of_done: Generated route starts and ends at the same snapped point
Route total distance is within requested distance ± margin
Route prefers unwalked edges over previously walked ones
Route achieves reasonable circularity rather than degenerating to out-and-back

steps.title: Route planner: A-to-B mode
steps.goal: Implement point-to-point route planning with diversity scoring and distance-dependent circularity weighting.
tasks: Snap both start and end points to nearest routable nodes
Implement A-to-B route search optimizing diversity penalty and circularity score
Implement circularity weight fading: reduce circularity weight as straight-line A-B distance approaches the requested walking distance
Enforce distance constraint: total route distance within requested distance ± margin
Return route as ordered list of edges, GeoJSON geometry, total distance, and scores
definition_of_done: Route starts at snapped A and ends at snapped B
Route distance is within requested distance ± margin
Circularity influence naturally fades when A-B straight-line distance is close to requested distance
Diversity scoring correctly penalizes previously walked edges

steps.title: Route planner: A-to-B shortest mode
steps.goal: Implement shortest-path routing from A to B that prefers unwalked edges within the shortest path margin.
steps.input: Graph data with pgRouting (step 7), diversity scoring (step 14)
tasks: Compute reference shortest path distance between snapped A and snapped B
Search for routes within shortest_path_distance × (1 + shortest_path_margin) that minimize diversity penalty
Apply diversity scoring to prefer unwalked edges among near-shortest alternatives
Return route with total distance, diversity score, and shortest-path reference distance
definition_of_done: Route goes from A to B within shortest path distance + shortest path margin
Route prefers unwalked edges when multiple near-shortest paths exist
Returns the shortest path itself when no alternatives exist within margin

steps.title: Route planner: escalation strategy
steps.goal: Implement the no-route-found escalation: incremental margin widening, single-turnaround fallback, and clear failure messaging.
tasks: Implement incremental margin widening: on failure, retry with progressively wider margin up to user's margin + 10 percentage points, hard ceiling of 100%
Determine and implement appropriate margin increment size (e.g., 5pp per retry)
Implement single-turnaround fallback: allow one segment to be doubled back, penalized as if walked twice in diversity scoring
Implement final failure response: clear message suggesting a different distance or starting point
Add metadata to route response indicating whether margin was auto-widened (and to what value) or a turnaround was used
definition_of_done: Planner retries with progressively wider margin before resorting to turnaround
Auto-widened margin never exceeds user's configured margin + 10pp or 100% absolute
Turnaround fallback produces a valid route with exactly one doubled segment penalized correctly
User is informed of all escalation measures via response metadata
Clear failure message returned when all escalation options exhausted

steps.title: Route planner: dead end and path preference handling
steps.goal: Integrate dead end avoidance and user path preference constraints into the route planner across all modes.
steps.input: Route planner modes (steps 16-18), user preferences (step 13), dead end flags (step 7)
tasks: When dead_ends_enabled is false, exclude dead-end edges from the traversable graph before planning
Handle start-in-dead-end edge case: if the starting node is in a dead end, allow routing out of the dead end, then apply dead end exclusion for the remainder
Apply blocked path preferences: fully exclude edges the user has blocked
Apply prefer_not penalty: use the calculated prefer_not multiplier in diversity scoring
Ensure all constraints integrate correctly with escalation strategy (step 19)
definition_of_done: Dead-end edges are excluded from routes when the setting is off
Starting from within a dead end correctly routes out before applying the constraint
Blocked paths never appear in generated routes
Prefer_not paths are used only when all alternatives are worse
Constraints work correctly across all three route modes and with escalation

steps.title: Walk tracking service
steps.goal: Implement server-side GPS trace processing, map-matching to graph edges, distance calculation, and walk completion detection.
tasks: Implement GPS trace ingestion: accept ordered GPS points with timestamps
Implement map-matching: snap GPS trace points to nearest graph edges, produce ordered list of walked edge stable IDs
Calculate walked distance from matched edges
Implement walk completion detection: check if latest position is within GPS accuracy distance of start point (circular) or end point (A-to-B modes)
Implement incremental trace processing: accept and process batches of new GPS points for an active walk session
Implement interrupted session handling: accept delayed bulk uploads and merge into existing session
Update edge_walk_counts and total_walked_distance via walk history service after processing
definition_of_done: GPS trace is correctly map-matched to graph edges
Edge walk counts update based on matched edges
Walk completion auto-detects based on proximity to the relevant endpoint
Incremental and interrupted trace uploads process and merge correctly
Total walked distance accumulates accurately in subscription record

steps.title: Subscription and free tier service
steps.goal: Implement free tier enforcement, subscription lifecycle management, and payment provider integration.
steps.input: Subscription schema (step 6), walk tracking for distance accumulation (step 21)
tasks: Implement free tier check: reject new route planning requests when user's total_walked_distance_m >= 100000
Allow an in-progress walk to continue and complete even after the limit is crossed mid-walk
Implement Mollie payment integration for web subscriptions (€10/year): create payment, handle webhook, activate subscription
Implement Apple IAP server-side receipt verification: validate receipt, activate subscription
Implement Google Play Billing server-side verification: validate purchase token, activate subscription
Implement subscription status query: return active/expired/free with remaining free distance
Handle subscription expiry: revert to free tier rules
Log all payment events to payment_events table
definition_of_done: Free tier blocks new route planning at 100km total walked
Active walk is not interrupted when crossing the free tier limit
Subscription activates after verified payment from any provider
Expired subscription correctly reverts to free tier enforcement
Payment events are logged for audit

steps.title: Rate limiting middleware
steps.goal: Implement per-user rate limiting on planning requests with increasing wait times for repeated rapid requests.
steps.input: Server scaffolding with middleware support (step 1), JWT auth middleware (step 9)
tasks: Implement rate limiter tracking planning requests per authenticated user per time window
Apply increasing backoff: each rapid successive request requires a longer wait
Return HTTP 429 with Retry-After header when rate limited
Scope rate limiting to planning endpoint only, leaving other endpoints unaffected
definition_of_done: Rapid repeated planning requests from the same user are throttled
Wait time increases with repeated violations within the window
Rate limit response includes Retry-After information
Non-planning endpoints are not affected by rate limiting

steps.title: Authentication API endpoints
steps.goal: Expose all authentication and account management operations as REST API endpoints.
tasks: POST /api/auth/register — email, password, username; returns JWT + refresh token
POST /api/auth/login — email, password; returns JWT + refresh token
POST /api/auth/refresh — refresh token; returns new JWT + refresh token
POST /api/auth/logout — revokes refresh token
POST /api/auth/social/google — Google authorization code; returns JWT + refresh token
POST /api/auth/social/apple — Apple identity token; returns JWT + refresh token
POST /api/auth/password-reset/request — email; sends reset email
POST /api/auth/password-reset/confirm — token, new password; resets password
DELETE /api/auth/account — requires confirmation body; deletes all user data immediately
Add input validation with clear error messages for all endpoints
definition_of_done: All endpoints return appropriate HTTP status codes and JSON responses
Input validation rejects malformed requests with descriptive errors
Protected endpoints require valid JWT in Authorization header
Account deletion requires explicit confirmation field in request body

steps.title: Route planning API endpoints
steps.goal: Expose route planning, re-planning, and point snapping as REST API endpoints with subscription and rate limit enforcement.
steps.input: Route planner with all modes and escalation (steps 16-20), rate limiting (step 23), subscription service (step 22)
tasks: POST /api/routes/plan — mode, distance_km, start_point, end_point (optional); returns route GeoJSON, total distance, scores, escalation metadata
POST /api/routes/snap — point coordinates; returns nearest routable point snapped to graph
Check subscription status before planning; return 402 with upgrade message if free tier exceeded
Apply rate limiting middleware to planning endpoint
Return route as GeoJSON LineString with ordered edge list, total distance, diversity score, circularity score, and escalation info
Return clear error response when server cannot produce a route after full escalation
definition_of_done: Planning endpoint accepts all three modes and returns valid route responses
Snap endpoint returns the nearest routable point
Free tier exceeded returns HTTP 402 with clear upgrade message
Rate-limited requests return HTTP 429 with Retry-After
Response includes escalation metadata when margin was widened or turnaround was used

steps.title: Walk tracking API endpoints
steps.goal: Expose walk lifecycle operations and edge walk count queries as REST API endpoints.
steps.input: Walk tracking service (step 21), walk history service (step 12)
tasks: POST /api/walks/start — walk session ID or route reference, mode; returns walk session ID
POST /api/walks/{id}/sync — array of GPS points; returns updated distance, matched edges, completion status
POST /api/walks/{id}/end — manual end of walk; marks session completed or abandoned with confirmation
POST /api/walks/{id}/resume — GPS points from interrupted session; merges into existing walk data
GET /api/walks/{id} — returns walk session details, progress, and status
GET /api/walks/edge-counts — bounds query parameter (bbox); returns per-edge walk counts within viewport for heatmap rendering
definition_of_done: Walk can be started, synced incrementally, and ended via API
GPS trace sync returns updated distance and auto-completion status
Interrupted session data can be uploaded and merged after delay
Edge count query returns walk counts within a bounding box for heatmap

steps.title: User preferences and profile API endpoints
steps.goal: Expose user settings, per-path preferences, and profile information as REST API endpoints.
tasks: GET /api/user/settings — returns all current user settings
PUT /api/user/settings — update margin, shortest_path_margin, dead_ends_enabled, circularity_weight; returns updated settings
PUT /api/user/path-preferences — edge_stable_id, preference (prefer_not or block); sets or updates preference
DELETE /api/user/path-preferences/{edge_stable_id} — removes preference
GET /api/user/path-preferences — optional bounds query parameter (bbox); returns preferences within viewport
GET /api/user/profile — returns username, subscription tier, subscription dates, remaining free tier distance
Restrict circularity_weight updates to the developer account only; reject with 403 for other users
definition_of_done: Settings endpoints read and update all user-configurable options
Path preferences can be set, queried by viewport bounds, and removed
Profile endpoint returns subscription status and calculated remaining free tier distance
Circularity weight update rejected with 403 for non-developer accounts

steps.title: Flutter project scaffolding
steps.goal: Initialize the Flutter project with app shell, navigation, theming, API client with JWT auth, and i18n-ready architecture.
tasks: Create Flutter project targeting iOS, Android, and web platforms
Set up navigation with two primary modes: planning mode and walking mode, with explicit mode switching
Implement app theming with a consistent design system
Structure all user-facing strings for i18n readiness (extractable, not inline)
Set up HTTP client for server API communication with automatic JWT injection in Authorization header
Implement secure token storage for access and refresh tokens (platform-appropriate secure storage)
Implement automatic token refresh on 401 responses
Set up state management approach (e.g., Riverpod, Bloc, or Provider)
definition_of_done: App builds and runs on iOS, Android, and web
Navigation between planning and walking modes works with explicit switching
API client makes authenticated requests with JWT and auto-refreshes expired tokens
All user-facing strings are in an i18n-ready structure

steps.title: Flutter: map widget and controls
steps.goal: Integrate the self-hosted tile server into a map widget with standard navigation controls and GPS position display.
tasks: Integrate a map library (e.g., flutter_map) configured to load tiles from the self-hosted tile server URL
Implement free pan and zoom gestures
Implement north reset button that reorients the map to north-up
Implement center-on-position button that re-centers the map on current GPS position
Display blue dot for current GPS position when GPS is available
Handle GPS unavailable gracefully: no blue dot shown, all other map controls remain functional
Ensure map renders and interacts correctly on iOS, Android, and web
definition_of_done: Map renders tiles from the self-hosted tile server
Pan, zoom, north reset, and center-on-position all function correctly
Blue dot appears at current GPS position when permission granted and GPS available
Map works fully without GPS except for blue dot and center-on-position

steps.title: Flutter: authentication screens
steps.goal: Build login, registration, social login, and password reset screens connected to the auth API.
steps.input: Flutter scaffolding with API client (step 28), auth API endpoints (step 24)
tasks: Build registration screen with email, password, and username fields; input validation; submit to API
Build login screen with email and password fields; submit to API; store tokens on success
Integrate Google Sign-In SDK; send authorization code to server social login endpoint
Integrate Sign in with Apple SDK; send identity token to server social login endpoint
Build password reset request screen (enter email, submit to API)
Build password reset confirmation screen (new password entry, activated via deep link with token)
Display clear error messages for all auth failure cases
On successful login: store tokens, navigate to main app; on logout: clear tokens, navigate to login
definition_of_done: User can register, log in, and log out with email and password
User can sign in with Google and Apple social login
Password reset flow works from request through email to new password confirmation
Auth state persists across app restarts via stored tokens

steps.title: Flutter: onboarding flow
steps.goal: Implement first-run onboarding that explains GPS usage and requests permission, with graceful degradation if denied.
tasks: Detect first-run state (no previous launch recorded)
Display onboarding screen explaining why GPS permission is needed (walk tracking and current position)
Request GPS/location permission via platform APIs
If permission denied: proceed to main app with degraded mode (no blue dot, no GPS-based start point, walk tracking unavailable)
Persist first-run completion so onboarding is not shown again
definition_of_done: First launch shows onboarding before the main app
GPS permission request includes clear explanation of purpose
App remains fully functional for planning and map viewing without GPS
Onboarding does not repeat on subsequent launches

steps.title: Flutter: planning mode UI
steps.goal: Build the planning mode interface with route mode selection, distance input, start/end point selection, margin display, and route request submission.
steps.input: Map widget (step 29), route planning API (step 25), auth state (step 30)
tasks: Build route mode selector: circular, A-to-B, A-to-B shortest
Build distance input control (kilometers)
Implement start point selection: use current GPS position if available, or allow tap on map; call snap API to snap to nearest routable point
Implement end point selection for A-to-B modes: tap on map, snap to nearest routable point via API
Display current margin and shortest path margin values as contextual information
Display remaining free tier distance when on free tier
Submit planning request to server; display returned route as a line on the map
Handle server unreachable: show clear error message, planning not possible
Display escalation info from server response (auto-widened margin, turnaround used, no route found with suggestions)
definition_of_done: User can select mode, enter distance, and pick start point (and end point for A-to-B modes)
Selected points snap to nearest routable path via API
Planned route displays on the map after successful server response
Margin values and free tier remaining are visible during planning
Server errors and no-route-found results display clearly to the user

steps.title: Flutter: history heatmap layer
steps.goal: Implement the walk history heatmap overlay for planning mode, showing per-edge walk counts with a color scale legend.
tasks: Fetch per-edge walk counts for the visible map viewport from the server edge-counts API
Render walked edges on the map colored by walk count using a heatmap color scale
Add a visual legend explaining the color scale
Display heatmap layer only in planning mode; hide it when switching to walking mode
Handle empty walk history: show a snackbar or overlay explaining what the heatmap will show once the user has walked
Optimize for performance: load edge data by viewport, debounce on pan/zoom
definition_of_done: Edges are colored by walk count in planning mode with correct heatmap coloring
Legend accurately explains the color scale
Heatmap layer is not visible in walking mode
Empty state shows explanatory message for users with no walk history
Rendering performs acceptably with many edges in the viewport

steps.title: Flutter: walking mode UI
steps.goal: Build the walking mode interface with route visualization, walked/unwalked coloring, off-route vibration, walk completion, and manual end.
tasks: Display the full planned route on the map as a polyline
Color the already-walked portion in a distinct color and the unwalked portion ahead in another
Implement off-route detection: compare current GPS position to route, vibrate device once per minute when off-route (threshold: GPS accuracy × 2)
Implement walk completion detection: trigger when user is within GPS accuracy of start point (circular) or end point (A-to-B)
For A-to-B modes: provide manual end walk button if auto-completion doesn't trigger
Implement manual early end at any time: show confirmation dialog, then end walk and preserve walked data
Ensure explicit mode switch required to enter/exit walking mode
No turn-by-turn navigation: display route line only
definition_of_done: Full planned route is visible with distinct colors for walked and unwalked portions
Off-route vibration triggers once per minute when the user deviates beyond threshold
Walk auto-completes when user arrives at the endpoint within GPS accuracy
Manual end walk works with confirmation and preserves all walked data
Mode switching between planning and walking is explicit

steps.title: Flutter: background walk tracking
steps.goal: Implement continuous GPS trace recording including background operation, local persistence, and server synchronization.
steps.input: Walking mode UI (step 34), walk tracking API (step 26)
tasks: Implement continuous GPS position recording during an active walk including when the app is backgrounded
Store GPS trace data locally on the device as it is recorded
Implement periodic batch sync of GPS trace to the server walk sync API
Handle app interruption (crash, force close, GPS loss): save trace locally, upload via resume API when app is reopened
Track every meter walked toward walk history and free tier distance
Disable walk tracking entirely on web platform; web supports planning and map viewing only
Handle GPS becoming unavailable during a walk: hide blue dot, continue recording if GPS resumes
definition_of_done: GPS trace records continuously including when app is in background
Trace data persists locally even if the app crashes or is force-closed
Locally stored traces upload successfully when the app is next opened
Walk tracking is disabled on web platform with no walk-related UI
Every walked meter is counted toward history and free tier usage

steps.title: Flutter: mid-walk re-planning
steps.goal: Enable the user to re-plan a route during an active walk from their current position with fresh parameters.
steps.input: Walking mode (step 34), planning mode UI (step 32), walk tracking (step 35)
tasks: Add a re-plan action accessible during an active walk
On re-plan: preserve the already-walked portion in history (synced to server)
Present the planning UI with fresh inputs: mode selection, distance input, end point selection for A-to-B modes
New route plans from the current GPS position as the start point
All three route modes are available for re-planning
Distance is entered fresh; no suggestion or auto-fill based on what was already walked
After new route is returned, walking mode resumes with the new route
definition_of_done: User can initiate re-planning at any point during a walk
Previously walked portion remains recorded in history
New route starts from current position with fresh mode and distance inputs
All three route modes work for re-planning
Walking mode continues with the new route after re-planning

steps.title: Flutter: user path preferences dialog
steps.goal: Build the map-based dialog for setting and removing prefer-not and block preferences on individual paths.
tasks: Create a dedicated map dialog screen accessible from settings as a global option (outside planning mode)
Implement tap-on-path interaction: identify the tapped edge and show preference options
Show options on tap: set prefer not, set block, or remove existing preference
Visually distinguish paths with preferences on the map (distinct styling for prefer-not vs blocked)
Fetch and display existing path preferences within the visible viewport from the API
Sync preference changes to the server immediately
Both prefer-not and block can be undone from the same dialog interface
definition_of_done: User can tap paths on the map to set prefer-not or block preferences
Existing preferences are visually distinct on the map
Preferences can be removed by tapping the same path
Changes persist to the server and affect future route planning

steps.title: Flutter: settings screen
steps.goal: Build the settings screen with margin controls, shortest path margin, dead ends toggle, and developer-only circularity slider.
steps.input: User settings API (step 27), auth state for developer detection (step 30)
tasks: Build settings screen accessible from main navigation
Add margin control (0–100%) for circular and A-to-B route distance tolerance
Add shortest path margin control (0–100%) for A-to-B shortest route tolerance
Add dead ends toggle (default: off — dead ends avoided)
Add circularity/diversity weighting slider: visible only when logged in as the developer account
Persist all changes to the server via the settings API
Add navigation link to the path preferences map dialog (step 37)
definition_of_done: All settings display current server-synced values and save changes on modification
Margin and shortest path margin accept values from 0 to 100%
Dead ends toggle defaults to off
Circularity slider is hidden for all non-developer accounts
Settings persist across sessions via server synchronization

steps.title: Flutter: profile and account screen
steps.goal: Build the profile screen displaying username, subscription status, and account deletion with immediate permanent deletion confirmation.
steps.input: Profile API (step 27), auth state (step 30), account deletion API (step 24)
tasks: Display username
Display current payment status and subscription tier (free/paid) with dates if applicable
Provide subscription management access (link to manage or cancel subscription)
Implement account deletion flow: confirmation step with clear message that deletion is immediate and permanent, removes all data including walk history, preferences, and payment status
On confirmed deletion: call DELETE /api/auth/account, then clear local state, log out, and navigate to login screen
Ensure identical deletion flow for email/password and social login users
definition_of_done: Profile displays username and correct subscription status
Subscription management is accessible from the profile
Account deletion confirmation clearly states immediacy and permanence
Successful deletion clears all local data and returns to login
Deletion flow works identically for all account types

steps.title: Flutter: payment and upgrade flow
steps.goal: Implement the freemium upgrade prompt and payment integration with Mollie (web), Apple IAP (iOS), and Google Play Billing (Android).
tasks: When user opens planning mode and free tier is exceeded: show upgrade prompt instead of planning UI
Implement Mollie payment flow for web platform (€10/year subscription)
Implement Apple In-App Purchase flow for iOS (€10/year subscription)
Implement Google Play Billing flow for Android (€10/year subscription)
After successful payment: refresh subscription status from server, unlock planning mode
Display remaining free tier distance in planning mode for free tier users
definition_of_done: Free tier exceeded correctly triggers upgrade prompt when entering planning mode
Payment completes successfully on web (Mollie), iOS (Apple IAP), and Android (Google Play)
Subscription activates after payment and planning is immediately unlocked
Remaining free tier distance is visible during planning for free tier users

steps.title: Server unit and integration tests
steps.goal: Implement comprehensive unit tests for scoring engines and services, and integration tests for all API endpoints.
steps.input: All server services and API endpoints complete (steps 1-27)
tasks: Unit tests for Fibonacci penalty calculation with edge cases (0 walks, high walk counts)
Unit tests for diversity scoring engine (penalty aggregation, normalization, prefer_not/block handling)
Unit tests for circularity scoring engine (perfect circle, out-and-back, irregular routes, normalization)
Unit tests for each route planner mode (circular, A-to-B, A-to-B shortest) with mock graph data
Unit tests for escalation strategy (margin widening bounds, turnaround penalty, exhaustion)
Unit tests for auth service (registration validation, password hashing, token lifecycle, social login linking, account deletion completeness)
Unit tests for walk tracking map-matching and distance calculation
Unit tests for subscription enforcement (free tier boundary, mid-walk crossing, expiry)
Integration tests for all API endpoints with a test database
Test edge ID stability: simulate OSM data refresh and verify IDs are preserved
definition_of_done: All unit tests pass
All integration tests pass with test database
Scoring algorithms have coverage for boundary conditions and edge cases
Auth flows tested including social login linking and complete account data deletion
Free tier enforcement tested at exact boundary (99.99km, 100km, 100.01km)

steps.title: Flutter widget and integration tests
steps.goal: Implement widget tests for all screens and integration tests for critical user flows.
steps.input: All Flutter screens and flows complete (steps 28-40)
tasks: Widget tests for authentication screens (registration validation, login, social login buttons, password reset)
Widget tests for planning mode UI (mode selection, distance input, point selection, error display)
Widget tests for walking mode UI (route display, walk controls, confirmation dialogs)
Widget tests for settings screen (all controls, developer-only slider visibility)
Widget tests for profile screen (subscription display, deletion confirmation)
Integration tests for full auth flow (register, login, logout)
Integration tests for plan-and-walk flow (select mode, plan route, start walk, complete)
Integration tests for re-planning during active walk
Verify web platform correctly omits walk tracking functionality
definition_of_done: All widget tests pass for every screen
Integration tests pass for auth, planning, and walking flows
Web platform integration test confirms walk tracking is absent
Critical user flow coverage ensures no regressions

steps.title: End-to-end system tests
steps.goal: Validate complete user journeys spanning the Flutter client and Rust server together.
steps.input: All server and client components complete and individually tested (steps 41-42)
tasks: E2E: register new account, plan a circular route, walk the route, verify walk history updates and heatmap reflects walked edges
E2E: social login, plan an A-to-B route, re-plan mid-walk, complete the new route, verify both walk portions in history
E2E: walk until free tier limit reached, verify planning is blocked, complete upgrade payment, verify planning is restored
E2E: set path preferences (prefer-not and block), plan a route, verify blocked path is absent and prefer-not path is deprioritized
E2E: delete account, verify all user data is removed from the database, verify login no longer works
E2E: simulate interrupted walk (app close), reopen app, verify partial walk data uploads and merges correctly
definition_of_done: All end-to-end test scenarios pass
User journeys verified across client and server interaction
Freemium boundary conditions tested at the 100km threshold
Account deletion verified as removing all data from all tables
Interrupted walk recovery verified with data integrity

steps.title: Deployment and CI/CD configuration
steps.goal: Set up production deployment for all infrastructure components and continuous integration pipeline.
tasks: Containerize the Rust/Axum server (Dockerfile, health check, graceful shutdown)
Configure PostgreSQL deployment with PostGIS and pgRouting extensions enabled
Configure tile server deployment with OSM data volume
Set up CI pipeline: run server unit and integration tests, Flutter widget and integration tests on every commit
Configure Flutter build pipelines for iOS (App Store), Android (Play Store), and web deployment
Set up production environment configuration (secrets management, database URLs, JWT keys, OAuth credentials, payment provider keys)
Document and configure OSM data refresh scheduling (periodic re-import with stable edge IDs)
Configure server retry behavior for transient connectivity failures to external services
definition_of_done: Server deploys, starts, and passes health check in production environment
Database migrations apply cleanly in production
Tile server serves tiles in production
CI pipeline runs full test suite on every commit and blocks on failure
Flutter apps build successfully for all three platforms
Production secrets are managed securely outside of source control
OSM data refresh process is documented with a runnable schedule
